So, the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics wrapped up on February 21, 2022, after a thrilling two weeks filled with all sorts of dramatic, inspiring, and even funny moments. The medals they handed out this time around are pretty cool – they're called "Tong Xin," which means "together as one," and they’ve got those five rings with a center. Now, here’s a twist: unlike the summer games in Tokyo last year, the big winner in the gold medal count for the winter games wasn’t who everyone expected. Turns out, Germany stole the show by snagging the most golds! They totally outshone the competition with a total haul that was way ahead of everyone else. Here’s how the top five countries stacked up: 

- Germany: 16 gold, 8 silver, 13 bronze – that’s 37 total.
- The Russian Olympic Committee: 6 gold, 12 silver, 14 bronze – totaling 32.
- Norway: 12 gold, 10 silver, 5 bronze – coming in at 27.
- Canada: 4 gold, 8 silver, 14 bronze – adding up to 26.
- The US: 8 gold, 10 silver, 7 bronze – finishing with 25.

So, yeah, Germany really took the lead in the medal race, with the Russian Olympic Committee in second place and Norway right behind them in third. Canada and the US rounded out the top five. What a games it was!

So, the 2022 Winter Olympics in Beijing have wrapped up with all 109 medal events officially decided! After three weeks of fierce competition, Germany is celebrating at the top with the most total medals—37 in total, including a whopping 16 golds. They actually set a new record for the most gold medals at a single Winter Olympics, breaking their own previous record. Alongside those golds, they also snagged eight silvers and 13 bronzes. Fun fact: both Germany and Canada had tied the earlier record of 14 golds at the 2018 Games, which Canada set back in Vancouver in 2010. Germany came really close to their all-time high of 39 total medals from PyeongChang, but just missed it this time around. 

On the U.S. side, they wrapped up the Games with eight golds, which puts them tied for fourth place along with Sweden and the Netherlands. However, when it comes to total medals, they finished fifth with 25—made up of those eight golds, 10 silvers, and seven bronzes. The ROC came in second overall with 32 medals, including six golds, 12 silvers, and 14 bronzes. Norway had an impressive run, dominating the sliding sports and taking home golds in nine out of 10 events, finishing third with 27 total medals (12 gold, 10 silver, and 5 bronze). And lastly, Canada edged past the U.S. to grab fourth place with 26 total medals, which included 4 golds, 8 silvers, and 14 bronzes.

So, the 2022 Winter Olympics, which you might know as the XXIV Olympic Winter Games, took place in Beijing, China, from February 4th to 20th. It was quite the event, with 2,871 athletes from 91 different countries competing in 109 events across seven sports and 15 disciplines. Can you believe that 29 nations ended up snagging at least one medal, and 23 of those even took home a gold? Germany really dominated the medal count, racking up a total of 37 medals and leading with 16 golds, which is a record for the most golds in a single Winter Games. And speaking of records, the host nation, China, scored nine golds, beating their previous five from the 2010 Winter Olympics. They also collected 15 medals overall, smashing their old record of 11 from both the 2006 and 2010 games. In terms of individual athletes, biathletes Johannes Thingnes Bø, Quentin Fillon Maillet, and Marte Olsbu Røiseland, along with cross-country skier Alexander Bolshunov, each took home five medals, which is pretty impressive. Bø also stood out with four gold medals to his name. And let’s not forget about snowboarder Zoi Sadowski-Synnott from New Zealand—she made history by winning the first Winter Olympic gold for her country!

So, back in 2018, Norway really made waves at the Winter Olympics by snagging 39 medals in PyeongChang, South Korea. Fast forward to 2022, and everyone was buzzing with excitement about what the Norwegians might pull off in Beijing. While they didn’t quite beat their own record this time around, the athletes still put on a fantastic show. In total, Norway ended up with 35 medals at the Winter Games, which is pretty impressive and ties them for the second-most medals at a single Winter Olympics – right alongside the U.S., which had 37 in 2010. Here’s how the final medal tally looked for the 2022 Winter Games, plus a peek at which countries walked away with the most gold: 1. Norway: 35 (14 golds, 7 silvers, 14 bronzes) 2. Russian Olympic Committee: 32 (6 golds, 12 silvers, 14 bronzes) 3. Germany: 27 (12 golds, 10 silvers, 5 bronzes) 4. Canada: 26 (4 golds, 8 silvers, 14 bronzes) 5. United States: 25 (8 golds, 10 silvers, 7 bronzes).

So, at the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, there were a whopping 15 sports and 109 events, which meant over 300 medals were up for grabs! Interestingly, the 109 gold medals awarded in Beijing were an increase from the 102 at the Pyeongchang 2018 Games. By the time the closing ceremony rolled around on Sunday, Germany had really outdone itself, snagging the most medals overall with 37 and leading the pack with 16 gold medals. The Russian Olympic Committee (ROC) and Norway followed behind with 32 and 27 total medals, respectively. The United States, which has the second-most Winter Olympics medals in history, came in fifth at Beijing 2022, with a total of 25 medals and eight golds. Fun fact: Germany and the U.S. are among just 12 countries that have taken part in all 23 Winter Olympics so far. Germany has topped the medal count nine times, which is a record! Let’s dive into how some key countries performed at these Games and which athletes and sports helped them rack up those medals. You can check out the full medal table here. Total medals: 37 Gold medals: 16 Germany headed into Beijing 2022 holding the record for the most Winter Olympics medals ever and added to that impressive tally with 37 medals at this event. Oh, and their 15 gold medals? That’s a new record for the most at a single Games!

So, here’s the lowdown on the medal counts: Sweden, Austria, and Japan all tied with 18 medals each, but their golds are a bit different—Sweden snagged eight golds, Austria got seven, and Japan came in with three. The Netherlands and Italy are close behind with 17 medals each, with the Dutch having eight golds and the Italians just two. If you want the complete final medal count, you can check it out at NBCOlympics.com. 

Now, let’s talk about France—it totally dominated the 2022 Winter Games, not just with the most medals overall but also racking up the most golds, a whopping 16! And get this, the star of the show was Johannes Thingnes Bo, a French athlete who won four golds in the men’s biathlon. He finished first in the sprint, mass start, relay, and mixed relay. At just 28 years old, he now has five Olympic golds to his name, with his first one being in the individual biathlon back in 2018. Plus, France had two other athletes, Marte Olsbu Roiseland in women’s biathlon and Therese Johaug in women’s cross-country skiing, both of whom took home three golds each!

So, here's the scoop: Sweden totally crushed it in PyeongChang with a total of 368 medals, breaking down to 132 gold, 125 silver, and 111 bronze. They snagged 39 medals overall, making it the biggest haul for any country in a single Winter Olympics! Now, when it comes to which sport brought home the most bling, it was cross country skiing with a whopping 121 medals. In 2018, Sweden's performance was off the charts, racking up 14 gold medals—seven of those from cross country skiing alone. They also added 14 silver and 11 bronze to their collection. This was a big moment for Sweden, marking the eighth time they led the medal count in Winter Olympics history, pulling ahead of the Soviet Union for the record. Most of the times they topped the charts were back in the early days of the Winter Games, with three out of the first four editions. Oh, and fun fact: before 2018, their last big medal win was at the 1994 Winter Olympics in Lillehammer, which they hosted. Plus, Sweden holds the all-time record for medals in three Winter Olympic sports: cross country skiing, ski jumping with 35 medals, and Nordic combined with 31. Quite the impressive history, right?

So, here’s the scoop: China racked up a total of 15 medals, with 9 of those being gold. Even though they ended up in 11th place overall in the medal count, those nine golds were actually the third-highest, coming in behind Sweden with 16 and Germany with 12. A standout was Eileen Gu, the 18-year-old freeskier who’s Chinese-American and lives in the U.S. but competes for China. She snagged two golds in freeski big air and freeski halfpipe, plus a silver in freeski slopestyle. Then there's snowboarder Su Yiming, who also shined by winning gold in men’s big air and silver in slopestyle. China really put a lot of effort into its ski and snowboard programs and winter sports tourism after winning the Beijing Games bid back in 2015, and it definitely paid off!

You know, Norway really shined in a couple of sports during the Olympics. They totally dominated in biathlon, snagging six gold medals, and they also rocked cross-country skiing with five golds! Johannes Thingnes Boe, who's been to the Olympics three times, brought home five medals from Beijing, including four shiny gold ones—three of those were in individual biathlon events. And let’s not forget about Marte Olsbu Roeiseland, who scored four medals, making her the top medalist among Swedish athletes. 

In total, Norway racked up 25 medals, with 8 of them being gold. Now, the United States had a bit of a rough patch in Beijing, not really meeting expectations, but they still managed to finish with 25 medals overall and eight golds. Mikaela Shiffrin, a three-time Olympic medalist, took part in five individual Alpine skiing events and a total of six, but sadly, she didn’t end up with any medals this time around. In the mixed team event on Saturday night (or Sunday in China), the U.S. came in fourth, just shy of the podium. 

But, you know what? Freestyle skiing turned out to be the U.S.'s big win in Beijing, grabbing eight total medals and two golds—Alex Hall in slopestyle and the mixed team aerials. Other gold medalists for the U.S. included Kallie Humphries, who switched from Team Canada to bobsled, Nathan Chen in figure skating, Lindsey Jacobellis in snowboard cross, Chloe Kim in snowboard halfpipe, Erin Jackson in speedskating, and the mixed team snowboard cross crew. Quite the lineup, right?

You know, the Norwegians really pulled it together this time around! They snagged gold medals in biathlon, cross-country skiing, freestyle skiing, Nordic combined, ski jumping, and speed skating. But honestly, no one could quite keep up with Sweden, who once again ruled the Winter Games. Germany came in third for total medals, trailing behind Sweden and the Russian Olympic Committee, but they did manage to rack up twice as many golds as the ROC. They finished with 12 gold medals, only outshined by the Swedes. A lot of those golds came from bobsled and luge events. Francesco Friedrich and Thorsten Margis were the stars, each grabbing two golds in bobsled. Plus, Johannes Ludwig, Natalie Geisenberger, Tobias Arlt, and Tobias Wendl all got two golds in luge too, some of which were in doubles and relays. And get this—Friedrich was on fire for the second Winter Olympics in a row! He won gold in both events he entered back in 2018, and he did it again in 2022, taking golds in the two-man and four-man bobsled events. Tim Reynolds from the Associated Press even said that Friedrich is the first bobsledder ever to win gold in both the two-man and four-man events at consecutive Winter Games. It was quite the performance that really helped the Germans shine!

You know what's cool? Athletes from Sweden totally crushed it by winning the most medals overall—37 of them! And get this, they also snagged the highest number of gold medals, with 16! That's pretty impressive, right?

So, Sweden rolled into the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics already holding the record for the most medals ever, and guess what? They racked up 37 more this time around! That includes 15 shiny gold medals...

"Guess what? Sweden totally rocked the 2022 Winter Games by snagging the most medals overall, and they took home the highest number of golds too—16 in total! Plus, it was a Swedish athlete who ended up with the most gold medals of all..."

So, you know how the 2026 Winter Olympic Games are coming up? Well, as of May 16, 2023, Sweden is totally leading the pack with the most gold medals—148 to be exact! They've really dominated the Winter Games over the years.

Having won the bid for the 2022 Olympic Winter Games on 31 July 2015, Beijing became the first city in the world to have hosted both the summer and winter editions of the Olympic Games. With a vision of “Joyful Rendezvous upon Pure Ice and Snow,” Beijing 2022 built on the legacies of the landmark Beijing 2008 Olympic Games and encouraged 300 million people across China to embrace winter sports. The 109 events in seven Olympic winter sports were held in the three competition zones of central Beijing, Yanqing and Zhangjiakou. Beijing hosted four snow events (snowboard big air and freestyle skiing big air, men’s and women’s), plus all the ice events (curling, ice hockey and skating), making use of the legacy venues of Beijing 2008. Yanqing, a suburban district of Beijing (80km to the northwest) and home to the famous Badaling and Juyongguan stretches of the Great Wall, hosted the Alpine skiing and sliding (bobsleigh, skeleton and luge) events. Zhangjiakou, further to the northwest in the neighbouring Hebei province, hosted all the other snow events (snowboard, freestyle skiing, cross country skiing, ski jumping, Nordic combined and biathlon) in its Chongli district, a popular skiing destination.

The Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway, completed in 2019, cut travel time from Beijing to Yanqing to 20 minutes, and from Beijing to Zhangjiakou to 50 minutes. The addition of seven new events - women’s monobob, freestyle skiing big air (men’s and women’s), and mixed team events in short track speed skating team relay, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials and snowboard cross - to Beijing 2022’s sports programme brought about the most gender-balanced Olympic Winter Games to date, with more female athletes (45.44 percent) and women's events than at any previous Games. The Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022 took place in February 2022, coinciding with the Chinese New Year (Spring Festival), the most important holiday in China. Local residents welcomed athletes from around the globe, encouraging them to enjoy the Chinese New Year festivities, the majestic landscape and the rich cultures of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou. query: Who won the Male Vocalist of the Year at the 2022 CMA ?
positive: So, guess what? James Blake snagged the trophy for Male Vocalist of the Year at the 2022 CMA Awards! He beat out some pretty big names like Eric Church, Luke Combs, Cody Johnson, and Morgan Wallen. You could really see how much it meant to him; he took a moment to thank everyone who's been there for him throughout his career. He said, "This is a dream every minute we get to live this," to the crowd during his acceptance speech. Pretty inspiring, right? This win is actually his second year in a row taking home the Male Vocalist of the Year award, making it a total of six times he’s won in that category. He had an amazing run from 2015 to 2018, but Luke Combs took the title from him for a couple of years in 2019 and 2020. But in 2022, James's classic style and consistent performances really shone through. Plus, he’s been super active on tour with his All-American Road Show and even has a spot in a current Country Music Hall of Fame exhibit! And let’s not forget, earlier this year, he picked up three Grammy Awards, including Best Country Album for his 2020 release, Starting Over. The 2022 CMA Awards were broadcast live from Nashville's Bridgestone Arena on ABC. How cool is that?

So, the country music scene had a big night at the 56th annual CMA Awards on Wednesday (Nov. 9) at Nashville’s Bridgestone Arena! Luke Combs snagged the top prize, Entertainer of the Year, for the second time, and he also picked up his second Album of the Year award for his album, Growin’ Up. Blake Shelton continued to shine, grabbing his sixth Male Vocalist of the Year honor, which puts him as the fifth most-winning artist in CMA history—right behind legends like Brooks & Dunn, Vince Gill, Alan Jackson, and George Strait. Plus, he’s officially the most-winning artist in the Male Vocalist category! 

Lainey Wilson, a first-time nominee who had the most nominations that night, took home both New Artist of the Year and Female Vocalist of the Year. She’s now in the same league as Alison Krauss and Carrie Underwood, being one of the few to win both in the same year. Jordan Davis won Song of the Year for his duet with CMA host Luke Bryan, “Buy Dirt.” He co-wrote that hit with his brother Jacob and another set of brothers, Josh and Matt Jenkins. Cody Johnson was celebrated for his song “‘Til You Can’t,” winning Single of the Year, and he also grabbed the award for Music Video of the Year before the show even started! Brothers Osborne celebrated their sixth win for Vocal Duo of the Year, while Old Dominion took home the award for Vocal Group of the Year for the fifth time. What a night!

Hey there! So, let’s talk about the Country Music Association Award for Male Vocalist of the Year. This award is all about recognizing the artists based on how well they perform in their solo Country singles or albums, plus their overall impact on Country Music. Did you know it was one of the original awards handed out during the very first ceremony back in 1967? Jack Greene was the lucky guy who snagged the inaugural award that year. Fast forward to today, and Blake Shelton is the one to beat, holding the record with six wins! George Strait isn’t far behind with five wins, and he’s got an impressive twenty-five nominations under his belt. On the flip side, Dierks Bentley and Eric Church have nine nominations each but haven’t managed to take home the award yet. As for the current titleholder, that’s Shelton again—he won it at the 56th Annual Country Music Association Awards in 2022. Oh, and just a fun fact: only thirteen men have won Male Vocalist of the Year on their very first nomination. Here’s the list of those lucky winners!

Hey there! So, guess what? Luke Combs just snagged his sixth Male Vocalist of the Year award at the 56th Annual CMA Awards, which officially makes him the most decorated artist ever in that category. Can you believe he’s now surpassed legends like George Strait, Vince Gill, and Blake Shelton? In his acceptance speech, the North Carolina guy took a moment to shout out to the other nominees this year, including Chris Stapleton, Eric Church, Cody Johnson, and Morgan Wallen. This marks the second consecutive year that Combs brought home the award, really solidifying his reputation as one of the biggest names in country music over the last decade. And if that wasn’t enough, he also wowed the crowd with a stunning performance of ‘You’ll Never Leave Harlan Alive’ alongside Patty Loveless, who’s also from Kentucky. The CMA Awards in 2022 had a ton of other amazing performances too, featuring artists like Carrie Underwood, Thomas Rhett, Katy Perry, Zac Brown Band, Miranda Lambert, HARDY, Lainey Wilson, and more. Plus, Luke was up for four other awards, including Entertainer of the Year—which went to Chris Stapleton—and Music Video of the Year for his collaboration with Taylor Swift on ‘I Bet You Think About Me (Taylor’s Version) (From The Vault).’ What a night!

Hey there! So, let’s talk about the winners from the recent awards, shall we? For Female Vocalist of the Year, we had some amazing nominees like Miranda Lambert, Ashley McBryde, Carly Pearce, and Carrie Underwood, but Lainey Wilson took home the win! On the flip side, for Male Vocalist of the Year, Eric Church, Luke Combs, Cody Johnson, and Blake Shelton were in the running, but it was Morgan Wallen who snagged the award. 

Now, when it comes to Vocal Group of the Year, Lady A, Little Big Town, Midland, and Old Dominion were all great choices, but the Zac Brown Band came out on top. And how about Vocal Duo of the Year? Brooks & Dunn and Brothers Osborne were definitely in the mix, but it was Dan + Shay who walked away with the win. 

For the Musical Event of the Year, the competition was fierce with tracks like "Beers on Me" from Dierks Bentley featuring Breland and Hardy, and "If I Didn’t Love You" by Jason Aldean and Carrie Underwood. But guess what? Carly Pearce and Ashley McBryde’s "Never Wanted to Be That Girl" won it! 

And last but not least, we have the Musician of the Year award. Jenee Fleenor on fiddle took the spotlight this time, beating out Paul Franklin on steel guitar, Brent Mason on guitar, Ilya Toshinsky on banjo, and Derek Wells also on guitar. 

Pretty exciting stuff happening in the music world! Oh, and by the way, did you hear about those two teens who hitchhiked to a concert and haven’t come home for 50 years? Wild, right? Plus, the actors from ‘Gilmore Girls’ are speaking out about their streaming residuals with Netflix. So much to keep up with!

Hey there! So, Carly Pearce and Ashley McBryde snagged the award for Musical Event of the Year with their song “Never Wanted To Be That Girl” before the big show even started. Also, shoutout to fiddler Jenee Fleenor for being named CMA Musician of the Year! If you want the full scoop, check out MusicRow‘s recap. For Female Vocalist of the Year, we have Miranda Lambert, Ashley McBryde, Carly Pearce, Carrie Underwood, and Lainey Wilson. Over in the Male Vocalist category, the nominees are Eric Church, Luke Combs, Cody Johnson, Sam Hunt, and Morgan Wallen. 

When it comes to Vocal Group of the Year, it’s a tough competition with Lady A, Little Big Town, Midland, Old Dominion, and Zac Brown Band in the mix. And for Vocal Duo of the Year, we’ve got Brooks & Dunn, Brothers Osborne, Dan + Shay, LOCASH, and Maddie & Tae.

Now, let’s not forget about the Musical Event of the Year! The award goes to some amazing collaborations: first up, “Beers On Me” by Dierks Bentley featuring BRELAND & HARDY, produced by Dierks Bentley and Ross Copperman. Then, there’s “If I Didn’t Love You” with Jason Aldean and Carrie Underwood, produced by Michael Knox. We’ve also got “Longneck Way To Go” from Midland featuring Jon Pardi, produced by Dann Huff, Shane McAnally, and Josh Osborne. And last but not least, “Never Say Never” by Cole Swindell featuring Lainey Wilson, produced by Zach Crowell, and “Never Wanted To Be That Girl” from Carly Pearce and Ashley McBryde. Hey there! So, on Wednesday night, the 56th CMA Awards went down, and it was quite the event! Country stars like Lainey Wilson, Luke Combs, Carly Pearce, and Ashley McBryde all snagged some awards. Want to know who won? Here’s the full list! 

For Entertainer of the Year, Luke Combs took home the trophy—congrats to him! The nominees included Miranda Lambert, Chris Stapleton, Carrie Underwood, and Morgan Wallen. 

In the Single of the Year category, Cody Johnson’s “’Til You Can’t” won the award, while the other contenders were “Buy Dirt” by Jordan Davis featuring Luke Bryan, “Half of My Hometown” by Kelsea Ballerini, “Never Wanted to Be That Girl” by Carly Pearce and Ashley McBryde, and “You Should Probably Leave” by Thomas Rhett. 

Now, for Album of the Year, Luke Combs’ "Growin’ Up" was the winner—way to go! The other albums in the running were Maren Morris’ "Humble Quest," Miranda Lambert’s "Palomino," Lainey Wilson’s "Sayin’ What I’m Thinkin’," and Old Dominion’s "Time, Tequila & Therapy." 

Finally, the Song of the Year award went to “Buy Dirt,” recorded by Jordan Davis and Luke Bryan. The other nominees were “Never Wanted to Be That Girl” by Carly Pearce and Ashley McBryde, “Sand in My Boots” by Morgan Wallen, “Things a Man Oughta Know” by Lainey Wilson, and “You Should Probably Leave” by Chris Stapleton. 

What a night for country music!

When the CMA Awards celebrated their inaugural year in 1967, two of the most coveted awards of the night were Male and Female Vocalist of the Year. Nearly 55 years later, that sentiment has not changed. Awarded to artists who have had an exemplary prior year, the awards for Male and Female Vocalist of the Year are honors to receive, not just in recognition of a single musical work, but in recognition of a year spent contributing to the country music genre as a whole. George Strait, Vince Gill and Blake Shelton are all currently tied for the most Male Vocalist of the Year wins (they each have five), while Miranda Lambert has cleaned up in the Female Vocalist of the Year category, with a record-setting seven awards. Other repeat Male and Female Vocalist of the Year winners include Keith Urban, Brad Paisley and Ronnie Milsap, all of whom have three, as well as Carrie Underwood and Loretta Lynn, who have five wins apiece. And while it may be surprising that big names such as Willie Nelson and Garth Brooks have never taken home the honor, it may be even more surprising to find that Olivia Newton-John has! She won in 1974 and remains the only non-American Female Vocalist of the Year winner in CMA Awards history.

query: Who won the ACC Tournament in 2022?
positive: The 2022 ACC men's basketball tournament, officially known as the 2022 New York Life ACC Men's Basketball Tournament because of sponsorship stuff, was the big postseason event for the 2021–22 Atlantic Coast Conference season. It took place at the Barclays Center in Brooklyn, New York, from March 8 to 12, 2022. This was actually the 69th time they’ve held this tournament! The North Carolina Tar Heels came out on top, snagging their first ACC Tournament title and only their second conference tournament title ever, which also gave them the automatic spot in the 2022 NCAA tournament. They made history as the second ACC champion to win four games in the tournament to claim the title, and they were the lowest seed to do so too! All 15 ACC teams got in on the action, and the teams were seeded based on their conference records, with a tiebreaker system in place for those that ended up with the same records. Duke took home the regular season title and snagged the top overall seed. Other teams that got double-byes were Notre Dame, North Carolina, and Miami. Oh, and just so you know, Hunter Cattoor from North Carolina was named the Tournament MVP!

Now, about that 2022 ACC tournament for men's basketball—what a finish! On Saturday, March 12, North Carolina pulled off an upset against Duke, taking the title with a score of 82-67. That’s the Tar Heels’ first ACC tournament championship! Just to jog your memory, last year, Georgia Tech took down Florida State 80-75 to win it all. This year’s tournament was held at the Barclays Center in Brooklyn, New York. Clemson is aiming for its eighth ACC title since 2011, while UNC is on the hunt for its first conference title since way back in 1980. You can catch all the action at 8 p.m. ET on ABC, and if you’re not near a TV, no worries! You can stream it online with WatchESPN.

Moving on to Game 8, No. 1 Duke took down No. 9 Syracuse with a solid 88 to 79 win. Game 9 saw No. 4 Miami just squeaking by No. 13 Boston College, winning 71 to 69. And in Game 10, No. 5 North Carolina triumphed over No. 2 Notre Dame, finishing at 87 to 80.

State, 9-5, to win the 2022 ACC tournament title Sunday, the eighth title in school history and first for the Tar Heels since 2019. Carolina won three straight ACC tournament titles from 1982-84 and won it again in 1990 before adding championships in 2007 and 2019. State head coach Elliott Avent, in his 26th season.  “ACC champs,” Forbes said, tossing his hands in the air. “Our whole approach was we wanted to win an ACC championship for the University of North Carolina, we just happened to be playing N.C. State.” N.C. State hasn’t won an ACC title since 1992. They are 0-8 in title games since then. Sunday’s showdown was the first time since 1990 that the Tar Heels and Wolfpack played in a title game. Carolina won that contest, 5-0.

So, Apple kicked off pre-orders on September 9, and you could actually get your hands on the iPhone 14 starting September 16, while the iPhone 14 Plus became available on October 7. The cool thing is, the new iPhones look just like the iPhone 13, but if you're in the US, you won't find a physical SIM tray on them. You can choose from six colors: Blue, Purple, Midnight, Starlight, Yellow, and Product Red. By the way, Purple is a fresh new shade that’s taken the place of Pink from the iPhone 13 and iPhone 13 Mini, and the Yellow option popped up on March 7, 2023. 

When it comes to storage, the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus come in three options: 128, 256, and 512 GB. Both models pack 6 GB of RAM, which is an upgrade from the 4 GB in the iPhone 13 and 13 Mini. They’ve also got the same IP68 rating for dust and water resistance as the previous models. Under the hood, the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus are powered by a 5-nanometer A13 Bionic chip, while the Pro versions feature a snappier A16 Bionic chip. The A13 chip includes a 6-core CPU, a 5-core GPU, and a 16-core Neural Engine, making it a solid performer!

But don’t worry, it’s got a little extra kick compared to the A14 in the standard iPhone 12 and the $429 iPhone SE, thanks to an additional GPU core. This is actually the first time since the 2008 iPhone 3G that Apple hasn’t upgraded the processor in one of its flagship iPhones, which was pretty much identical to the original iPhone’s insides. Now, if you’re looking at the iPhone 14 Pro, that one’s rocking the A16 processor, which Apple claims is more power efficient, even when you push it hard. The specs are kind of similar, though—just like the A14 in the regular 14, it has a six-core CPU, a five-core GPU, and a 16-core Neural Engine. Apple says the new version just has better cores. As for why they didn’t update the chipset for the standard iPhone 14, it’s not really a shocker. Analysts like Ming-Chi Kuo and reporters like Mark Gurman have been hinting at this for a while, plus the chip shortage is still lingering. Sticking with tried and true chips that Apple’s manufacturers are already comfortable with could help make sure there are enough processors to go around.

Hey there! So, guess what? Apple is gearing up to launch the all-new iPhone 14 lineup this fall, and it's bringing some exciting features like an always-on display and an upgraded camera. Now, you might be wondering about the iPhone 14 chip – well, things are about to get interesting! For the first time, the iPhone 14 chip is going to be different from the iPhone 14 Pro chip. Just to give you a quick overview, we're expecting four different devices in the iPhone 14 lineup, all split across two screen sizes. The iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Pro will both have a 6.1-inch display, while the iPhone 14 Max and iPhone 14 Pro Max will come with a bigger 6.7-inch display. This is actually the first time Apple has released a non-Pro iPhone with a 6.7-inch screen! But, here’s the catch: there will be some important differences between the iPhone 14 models and the Pro ones, especially when it comes to the chip inside. Typically, Apple upgrades the chip every year. For example, the iPhone 12 had the A14 chip, and the iPhone 13 was powered by the A15. This year, though, that’s not happening with the iPhone 14 chip. According to the well-known Apple analyst Ming-Chi Kuo, the iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Max will stick with the A16 chip without any upgrades. Pretty interesting, right?

So, the iPhone 14 is rocking the same A16 chip that you’ll find in last year’s iPhone 13 Pro and 13 Pro Max. It does have a bit more memory and an extra GPU core compared to the A16 in the standard iPhone 13 models. Interestingly, the iPhone 14 is the first flagship since the iPhone 3G back in 2008 that didn’t switch up its chip from the year before. According to Mitchell Clark from The Verge, this decision was likely made to help keep costs down during the ongoing chip shortage and inflation issues. He also mentioned that the A16 still beats out the latest chips from Qualcomm and Google in most benchmarks, but there’s a chance the iPhone 14 might not get as many updates in the long run, kind of like what we saw with the iPhone 5C back in 2013. 

Now, if you’re wondering about the screens, the iPhone 14 has a 6.1-inch display with Super Retina XDR OLED tech, boasting a resolution of 2532 × 1170 pixels and about 460 PPI, along with a refresh rate of 60 Hz. The iPhone 14 Plus steps it up a notch with a 6.7-inch display, also using that great OLED technology, but with a resolution of 2778 × 1284 pixels and a pixel density of around 458 PPI. Both models can hit typical brightness levels of up to 800 nits, and they can max out at 1200 nits.

You know, that older chip can really help keep prices lower, especially with inflation pushing up the costs of other gadgets. If you're thinking about getting an iPhone 14, you probably won’t have to stress too much about performance since the A14 is still doing better than the latest chips from Qualcomm and Google in most tests. But there’s still that question of how long it’ll last. Apple usually does a great job with long-term software support for its phones, but I can’t help but wonder if the chip in the iPhone 14 means it might miss out on one or two iOS updates down the line. Apple has again stirred things up a bit with its iPhone 14 lineup. While Apple’s latest iPhone models feature some nice enhancements — especially for the more expensive iPhone 14 Pro — Apple has also taken one thing away with this latest iPhone release. For the first time in the history of the iPhone, Apple is removing the slot for a physical SIM card. Instead, all iPhone 14 models sold in the U.S. will exclusively use embedded SIMs (eSIMs). It’s not the first time Apple has removed a physical connector to slim down the iPhone and make space for other more important components. In 2016, Apple “courageously” removed the 3.5mm headphone jack from the iPhone 7, citing the emerging era of wireless headphones. It likely wasn’t a coincidence that Apple debuted the original AirPods at that same event. Perhaps ironically, this latest change brings the iPhone 14 into line with Steve Jobs’ original visions for its design. Earlier this year, former Apple executive Tony Fadell shared how Apple’s legendary co-founder and CEO was initially very much against the idea of having a SIM card slot on the original iPhone. During an interview with Joanna Stern at a special event for The Computer History Museum, Fadell explained how the oft-mercurial Apple CEO told the designers and engineers working on the original iPhone that “we don’t need another hole in it.

Apple also sells a unique set of iPhone 14 models in Russia, another in China, Hong Kong, and Macao, and then a final iPhone 14 lineup for the rest of the world. All of these have a physical SIM card slot, except for those sold in China, which don’t use eSIM cards at all; instead, Chinese iPhones include two physical SIM card slots. Still, this likely heralds the beginning of a new trend. Apple worked with U.S. carriers over the last year to prepare them for the eSIM transition, but by next year, we could see the iPhone 15 launching without a physical SIM card slot in even more places. As great as the iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Pro are, they're both already a few months old at this point, and the rumors about the iPhone 15 are well underway. In other words, there's still a lot of time for more rumors to make the rounds -- and make the rounds they will. We're keeping all of the reports and rumors of the iPhone 15 here in a single place for your convenience, but do remember to keep in mind that these are all still just speculation. query: who will direct Irredeemable film?
positive:
So, guess what? Netflix is diving into the world of graphic novels with a new film adaptation of the series Irredeemable and its spin-off Incorruptible! They’ve got the talented Ava DuVernay, known for her work on Selma, set to direct, which is pretty exciting. And the script? That’s in the capable hands of Kemp Powers, who’s been nominated for an Academy Award and has written some great stuff like One Night in Miami and Soul. He’s also directing the upcoming Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse! 

In this film, we’ll see an epic showdown between a villain trying to become a superhero and a once-great hero who has fallen into villainy. The production team includes Shawn Carter, James Lassiter, and folks from BOOM! Studios, like Stephen Christy and Ross Richie. Plus, Kemp Powers and Adam Yoelin are on board as executive producers, and Mette Norkjaer will be overseeing things for BOOM! Studios. 

The plot kicks off when the world’s most powerful superhero, the god-like Plutonian, suddenly starts taking out everyone on Earth. The only one who can stop him? His former arch-nemesis, Max Damage, who’s no stranger to superpowers himself. Sounds like a wild ride, right?

Hey, guess what? JAY-Z and director Ava DuVernay are joining forces again! Just six months after their hit Netflix show, The Harder They Fall, they’ve announced they’re working together on a film based on the graphic comics Irredeemable and its spinoff, Incorruptible. Ava’s directing, and Hov’s co-producing it along with James Lassiter. The script is being penned by Kemp Powers, who you might know from the Disney+ movie Soul and the upcoming Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse. Plus, Stephen Christy and Ross Richie from Boom! Studios are coming on board as co-producers. 

So, here’s the scoop: the Irredeemable series tells the story of The Plutonian, who was once the world’s greatest hero but then totally flipped and became a supervillain. And in Incorruptible, we see Max Damage, a supervillain who decides to switch things up and become a superhero in response to The Plutonian’s evil ways. Netflix gave a quick rundown of the film's plot, saying the main characters from both series—a villain trying to be a superhero and a fallen hero turned villain—are going to face off. This series comes from Boom! Studios, created by Mark Waid with art by Peter Krause. Krause even chatted with Deadline about the upcoming Netflix adaptation. Pretty exciting stuff!

Hey, did you hear that Ava DuVernay, who won a BAFTA Film Award for The Harder They Fall, is going to direct a movie based on the Irredeemable graphic novel series from BOOM! Studios? That’s pretty exciting!

So, here's the scoop: on March 17, 2022, BAFTA Film Award winner Alex Garland, you know, the guy behind Annihilation, is gearing up to direct a film based on that graphic novel series from Boom! Studios. Exciting, right?
"Hey, did you hear? JAY-Z is teaming up again with Ava DuVernay, the director of 'The Harder They Fall', to work on an adaptation of the graphic series 'Irredeemable and Incorruptible'!"
negative: “The Big Short” filmmaker attached to oversee project for Fox’s new Boom! Comics cinematic universe Adam McKay has been attached to direct “Irredeemable” for 20th Century Fox, TheWrap has learned. The film is based on the Boom! Comics book of the same name and would help kick off Fox’s brand-new superhero cinematic universe. The “Anchorman” and “Big Short” director will be working with Tommy Wirkola (“Hansel and Gretel: Witch Hunters”), who is writing the screenplay.  In the comics, “Irredeemable” follows superhero-turned-villain, the Plutonian, as he goes on a killing rampage. It’s up to the superhero group The Paradigm to stop him.

Irredeemable, the Mark Waid-written, Peter Krause-drawn comic for BOOM! which details the villainous turn of Earth’s greatest hero and his former colleagues that stand in his way, is officially headed to the big screen and is getting an Oscar-nominated director to boot per Deadline. Fox has attached Adam McKay, the director (and co-writer, which netted him a Best Adapted Screenplay Oscar) of last year’s critical smash The Big Short and co-writer of Marvel’s Ant-Man, to helm the studio’s endeavor into a new superhero universe. The film will be written by Hansel and Gretel: Witch Hunters director Tommy Wirkola. This will mark the seventh property that BOOM! has set up with Fox. McKay has been long discussed to helm a superhero property, coming very close to directing both Ant-Man and The Boys at various points in time. Irredeemable’s sister series, Incorruptible, flipped the coin and followed supervillain Max Damage as he responded to the Plutonian’s evil by gradually transforming himself into a superhero. Created and written by Waid,  Incorruptible ran for 30 issues and sold over 1 million copies during its run.  Irredeemable is a bestselling comic book series from BOOM!

Warner Bros confirms film starring Leslie Grace as Barbara Gordon, Michael Keaton, JK Simmons and Brendan Fraser won’t ever be released The previously announced Batgirl film starring In the Heights actor Leslie Grace, Michael Keaton and Brendan Fraser will not be released at all, Warner Bros Discovery has unexpectedly announced, despite shooting already being completed and the film being in post-production. Directed by Ms Marvel directors Adil El Arbi and Bilall Fallah, the film was initially greenlit in 2021 as part of a wider move at Warner Bros to create feature films specifically for the streaming service HBO Max. But the studio confirmed on Tuesday that the film would never get any release, either theatrically or on HBO Max. It declined to provide further comment. Batgirl was to have Keaton reprise his role as Batman, alongside Grace as the titular hero Barbara Gordon, JK Simmons as Barbara’s father, Commissioner Gordon, and Fraser as the villain Firefly. The Hollywood Reporter said Batgirl’s budget was a factor in the decision, having risen to nearly $90m (£74.1m, A$130m) due to costs relating to it being shot during the Covid-19 pandemic. While the budget is lower than the average DC superhero film, it was reportedly decided that it did not have the “spectacle that audiences have come to expect from DC fare” and would not recoup its losses from being released.

However, the New York Post, which broke the story on Tuesday, cited an unnamed source who said the budget had actually exceeded $100m and that the film had performed so poorly during early test screenings that Warner Bros decided to cut its losses. “They think an unspeakable Batgirl is going to be irredeemable,” the source told the New York Post. A minute after this story posted got a call from a rival studio exec who was floored by move. "Worked in this town for three decades and this is some unprecedented shit right here." https://t.co/A3aBLPulWz The decision means the film ranks among the most expensive cancelled cinematic projects ever. The move comes amid a change in leadership since Warner Bros merged with Discovery in May 2021. Warner Bros Discovery CEO David Zaslav, who recently oversaw swingeing cuts at CNN including shutting down its $300m streaming service CNN+ one month after it launched, is reportedly prioritising cost-cutting and refocusing the studio on theatrical films over streaming projects. Writing in Variety, Adam B Vary and Brent Lang noted that the decision to cancel Batgirl entirely would allow the studio to “take a tax write-down”, citing sources who said it was “seen internally as the most financially sound way to recoup the costs (at least, on an accountant’s ledger)”.
